questions.question: Analyse why sexual reproduction leads to more variations than asexual reproduction.
questions.answer: Sexual reproduction combines genes from two parents via gamete fusion, crossing over (recombination), and independent assortment, generating novel combinations. Asexual relies on mitotic copying with only rare mutations, producing near-identical offspring.

questions.question: Explain the chromosomal explanation for Mendel's laws of segregation and independent assortment.
questions.answer: Segregation: Homologous chromosomes separate in anaphase I of meiosis. Independent assortment: Different pairs align randomly at metaphase I. This produces genetic ratios matching Mendel's observations.

questions.question: Discuss codominance and multiple alleles using ABO blood group system.
questions.answer: Codominance: IA and IB both expressed (AB group). Multiple alleles: IA, IB, i control groups. Inheritance shows all four phenotypes possible depending on parental genotypes.

questions.question: How is pedigree analysis useful for human genetic studies? Illustrate with examples.
questions.answer: Pedigrees map inheritance patterns across generations. Identify dominant (every generation), recessive (skips), sex-linked (male bias) modes. Example: Haemophilia pedigree reveals X-linked recessive pattern.

questions.question: Explain incomplete dominance with a suitable example and contrast with complete dominance.
questions.answer: Incomplete dominance: Blending phenotype (e.g., red × white flowers → pink F1; F2 1:2:1). Complete dominance: One trait masks other (e.g., tall hides dwarf).

questions.question: Analyse the statement: 'Variations are small in asexual reproduction but significant in sexual.'
questions.answer: Asexual: Minor variations from DNA errors. Sexual: Major from recombination, assortment, two parents. Significant variation in sexual aids adaptation and evolution.

questions.question: Explain why some traits are more common in males for sex-linked disorders.
questions.answer: X-linked recessive traits: Males (XY) express if one X carries allele. Females (XX) need both. Hence, colour blindness/haemophilia more frequent in males.

questions.question: Discuss the evolutionary significance of genetic variation.
questions.answer: Variation supplies raw material for natural selection. Adaptive variations increase fitness, accumulate over time, leading to speciation and biodiversity.

questions.question: Explain how gene-environment interaction determines phenotype.
questions.answer: Genotype provides blueprint; environment modulates expression. Example: PKU disorder harmful only with phenylalanine diet; nutrition affects height potential.

questions.question: Analyse Mendel's choice of pea plants for his experiments.
questions.answer: Pea plants self-pollinating (pure lines easy), short life cycle, contrasting traits, large offspring numbers, easy cultivation and cross-pollination control.
